profile.vibe: Cultural immersion, neighborhood crawls, local not tourist
profile.activities: Daytime: neighborhood crawls, art walks, offbeat museums, outdoor, markets

restaurants.name: Kasama
restaurants.description: World's only Michelin-starred Filipino restaurant. Brunch is more accessible — ube pancakes, longanisa, lumpia. Dinner is a prix fixe that'll change how you think about Filipino food forever.
restaurants.rationale: This is THE reason Chicago's Filipino scene is having a moment. Chef's-pick paradise — trust the tasting menu completely. Nothing remotely like this exists downstate or almost anywhere else on Earth.

restaurants.name: HaiSous Vietnamese Kitchen
restaurants.description: Bib Gourmand Vietnamese that goes way beyond pho. Bo luc lac, whole roasted catfish, shaking beef. The flavors are precise and bold — this is what happens when a Vietnamese chef cooks for a Vietnamese audience.
restaurants.rationale: Elevated Vietnamese without losing soul. Chef Thai Dang's menu is a trust-the-kitchen dream. Bib Gourmand for a reason. Pairs perfectly with a Pilsen mural walk.

restaurants.name: Birrieria Zaragoza
restaurants.description: Bib Gourmand birria specialist — weekends only for the signature lamb birria. The Zaragoza family cooks one thing and cooks it perfectly. Consomé with your birria is mandatory.
restaurants.rationale: Weekend-only birria pilgrimage. Bib Gourmand. One family, one dish, perfected over decades. This is the kind of restaurant that food writers fly to Chicago for. Trust the only thing on the menu.

restaurants.name: Demera Ethiopian Restaurant
restaurants.description: Michelin-noted Ethiopian — communal injera platters, doro wat, kitfo (raw beef tartare if you dare), tibs. Eat with your hands from a shared platter. The veg combo is legendary.
restaurants.rationale: Full cultural immersion dining. Hands-on eating, shared platters, flavors that don't exist in central/southern IL. Kitfo is the adventurous pick; veg combo is the gateway. Both are incredible.

restaurants.name: Sabri Nihari
restaurants.description: Bib Gourmand Pakistani — the nihari (slow-braised beef shank stew) is cooked overnight and might be the single best $12 dish in Chicago. Bone marrow melting into spiced gravy. Haleem is a close second.
restaurants.rationale: Bib Gourmand at street-food prices. The nihari is a legendary Chicago dish. Devon Avenue itself is the experience — pair this with a full corridor walk. Spice levels are real here.

restaurants.name: The Gundis Kurdish Kitchen
restaurants.description: One of very few Kurdish restaurants in America. Michelin-recommended. Weekend brunch is a revelation — shakshuka, lamb kebabs, Kurdish breakfast spread. The owners fled Iraq and cook from homeland memory.
restaurants.rationale: Kurdish food literally doesn't exist outside major metros. The story behind this restaurant is as powerful as the food. Weekend brunch is the move — the full Kurdish spread is a trust-the-kitchen moment.

restaurants.name: Ghin Khao Eat Rice
restaurants.description: Bib Gourmand Isan Thai — this is NOT your local pad thai shop. Larb with toasted rice powder, som tum with fermented fish sauce, khao soi. The chef cooks from northeastern Thailand and doesn't dumb it down.
restaurants.rationale: Bib Gourmand. Regional Isan Thai that most Americans have never experienced. The spice is real and the flavors are complex. Burns-it-down spice level meets chef's-pick philosophy — order the larb and khao soi.

restaurants.name: MCCB (Mala Chinese BBQ)
restaurants.description: Whole fish swimming in a sea of chili oil, Szechuan peppercorns, and garlic. The mala (numbing-spicy) profile will rewire your taste buds. Dry pot and BBQ skewers are equally insane.
restaurants.rationale: Pure Szechuan heat and ma la numbness. This is where your burn-it-down spice tolerance meets its match. The whole fish is a showpiece and the dry pot is legit. Chinatown walkabout essential.

restaurants.name: San Soo Gab San
restaurants.description: Old-school Korean BBQ with charcoal grills at every table and 25+ banchan side dishes. You cook your own galbi and bulgogi over real charcoal — the interactive dining experience is the point.
restaurants.rationale: BYOB + tabletop charcoal grilling + 25 side dishes = a meal that IS the activity. Interactive, social, and the kind of experience your sister will remember. This is daytime-doable for late lunch.

restaurants.name: Carnitas Uruapan
restaurants.description: Giant copper cazo out front slow-cooking pork all day. Order carnitas by the pound — crispy, juicy, simple. This is a Pilsen institution. Cash only, no frills, legendary pork.
restaurants.rationale: Cash-only Pilsen legend. The carnitas are cooked in a traditional copper cazo visible from the street. Buy by the pound, eat with fresh tortillas. Combine with Pilsen mural walk and National Museum of Mexican Art.

restaurants.name: Boonie's
restaurants.description: Bib Gourmand next-gen Filipino. Sisig, lechon kawali, kare-kare — the menu bridges heritage recipes with modern technique. More accessible than Kasama if you want Filipino without the tasting menu commitment.
restaurants.rationale: Bib Gourmand Filipino as the accessible entry point to Chicago's Filipino explosion. If Kasama is the splurge, Boonie's is the 'trust the kitchen at any price point' pick. Great for a casual lunch.

restaurants.name: Phoenix Restaurant
restaurants.description: Classic dim sum with cart service — the real deal. Har gow, siu mai, chicken feet, turnip cake, egg tarts. Point at what looks good from the carts rolling past. Weekend brunch ritual.
restaurants.rationale: Cart-service dim sum is a dying art form. Phoenix is one of the last places in Chicago doing it. Trust the carts — point at what intrigues you. Chicken feet is the adventurous pick. Weekend mornings are the move.

restaurants.name: Maman Zari
restaurants.description: One of very few Persian tasting-menu restaurants in the country. 9 courses of saffron-laced rice, herb stews, kebabs, and Persian desserts. Intimate setting, family-run, feels like being invited to someone's home.
restaurants.rationale: Persian tasting menu at a fraction of fine-dining prices. The chef's-pick ethos is literally the format — you eat what Maman makes. Saffron rice, slow-cooked stews, flavors that are aromatic not spicy. Ultra-rare cuisine format.

restaurants.name: Thattu
restaurants.description: Kerala street food that got NYT recognition. Dosas, appam, fish curry, beef fry — South Indian food that's completely different from the North Indian restaurants next door on Devon. Run by Chicagoans of Kerala descent.
restaurants.rationale: NYT-recognized Kerala food right on the Devon corridor. Combine with Sabri Nihari and a full Devon Ave walk for a South Asian immersion afternoon. The beef fry and fish curry bring the heat you want.

restaurants.name: Kasia's Polish Deli
restaurants.description: 4x 'Best Pierogi in Chicago.' Hand-pinched pierogi, hunter's stew (bigos), fresh kielbasa, potato pancakes. Counter-service deli that feels like stepping into a Polish grandmother's kitchen.
restaurants.rationale: Chicago has the largest Polish population outside Warsaw — Kasia's is the distillation of that heritage. Quick, cheap, outstanding pierogi. Combine with a Ukrainian Village neighborhood walk.

restaurants.name: Galit
restaurants.description: Michelin-starred modern Middle Eastern from chef Zach Engel. Wood-fired pita, lamb shawarma, beet hummus, duck kibbeh. Every dish is a reinvention of a classic — beautiful plating, bold flavors.
restaurants.rationale: Michelin star. Modern Middle Eastern that pushes boundaries while respecting roots. Trust the chef completely here — the duck kibbeh and whole roasted cauliflower are signature. This is your splurge meal.

activities.name: Pilsen Mural Walk + National Museum of Mexican Art
activities.description: Self-guided walk through 50+ murals in Pilsen's streets, ending at the National Museum of Mexican Art (free admission, always). The murals tell the story of Mexican-American life, immigration, and resistance.
activities.rationale: Pilsen is one of America's greatest outdoor galleries. Combine with Carnitas Uruapan or HaiSous for a full half-day. The museum is genuinely world-class and always free.

activities.name: Devon Avenue South Asian Corridor Walk
activities.description: A mile-long strip of Indian, Pakistani, Nepali, and Bangladeshi shops, restaurants, and groceries. Sari shops, Bollywood music stores, fresh jalebi from sweet shops, and the best subcontinental food outside the subcontinent.
activities.rationale: A full sensory immersion afternoon. Eat at Sabri Nihari, browse Indian groceries, try jalebi from a sweet shop, pick up spices. This is the 'I'm not in Illinois anymore' experience. Pair with Thattu.

activities.name: Chinatown Food & Culture Walk
activities.description: Explore the main strip on Wentworth Ave plus the underground food court in Richland Center. Dim sum at Phoenix, Szechuan at MCCB, browse tea shops, herbalists, and bakeries for egg tarts and pineapple buns.
activities.rationale: Chicago's Chinatown is walkable, dense with food, and full of surprises. The underground food court in Richland Center is the hidden gem. Pair dim sum brunch at Phoenix with a MCCB spice bomb lunch.
activities.hours: Most shops 10am-8pm; dim sum starts at 8am
activities.pairing: Phoenix, MCCB, Happy Lamb Hot Pot

activities.name: International Museum of Surgical Science
activities.address: 1524 N Lake Shore Dr, Chicago, IL 60610
activities.description: A museum of surgery history in a lakefront mansion. Trepanning tools, iron lungs, antique surgical instruments, and a recreated 19th-century pharmacy. Beautifully weird.
activities.rationale: Genuinely one-of-a-kind museum. If you want offbeat, this is the ultimate. The building itself (a Gold Coast mansion) is gorgeous, and the exhibits range from fascinating to 'I can't believe I'm looking at this.' Lakefront walk after.
activities.pairing: Lakefront walk south toward Navy Pier area (skip the pier itself)

activities.name: Chicago Riverwalk Architectural Kayaking
activities.description: Kayak the Chicago River through the canyon of skyscrapers. Guided architecture tours from the water — you paddle past Marina City, the Wrigley Building, and under Michigan Ave. Way better than the tourist boat.
activities.rationale: Architecture from the water, but YOU'RE paddling. Active, outdoor, and the perspective is completely different from the boat tours. Morning slots are calmer water. Great start to a day.
activities.pairing: Walk to Chinatown after (1 mile south)

activities.name: Maxwell Street Market
activities.description: Sunday-only open-air market since 1912. Mexican street food stalls (elote, tacos, tamales, aguas frescas), vintage goods, tools, produce. The food stalls alone are worth the trip — it's a Sunday morning institution.
activities.rationale: A Chicago institution since 1912. The food stalls are legendary — get a Maxwell Street Polish (pork sausage with grilled onions and mustard) and elote. Sunday morning ritual. Gritty, authentic, alive.
activities.pairing: Walk to Pilsen after (15 min south)

activities.name: The 606 / Bloomingdale Trail
activities.description: Elevated rail trail converted to a 2.7-mile walking/biking path. Runs through Wicker Park, Bucktown, Humboldt Park, and Logan Square — four of Chicago's coolest neighborhoods. Public art installations along the way.
activities.rationale: Chicago's answer to the High Line but less crowded and through actually interesting neighborhoods. Walk it west to east and drop down into Logan Square or Wicker Park for food. Free, beautiful, great people-watching.
activities.pairing: Boonie's (Wicker Park end) or Logan Square coffee shops

activities.name: Bronzeville Heritage Walk
activities.description: Walk through the historic 'Black Metropolis' — the neighborhood that was home to the Great Migration, Louis Armstrong, and the birth of Chicago blues. Victory Monument, Muddy Waters' old haunts, DuSable Museum nearby.
activities.rationale: Deep American history you can't get from a textbook. The Great Migration story is written in these streets. Pair with the DuSable Black History Museum. This is cultural immersion at its most meaningful.
activities.pairing: DuSable Museum, then Chinatown (10 min north)

activities.name: Garfield Park Conservatory
activities.address: 300 N Central Park Ave, Chicago, IL 60624
activities.description: One of the largest conservatories in the nation — free to enter. Palm house, fern room, desert room, and an outdoor sensory garden. Feels like walking into a different climate inside each room.
activities.rationale: Free, gorgeous, and almost never on tourist lists. The palm house is jaw-dropping. Great morning stop before heading to the 606 trail (nearby) or a neighborhood crawl. Locals love it, tourists miss it.
activities.pairing: The 606 trail (1 mile east), then Wicker Park for food

activities.name: Argyle Street / Little Saigon Walk
activities.description: Vietnamese corridor along Argyle Street — pho shops, banh mi bakeries, tea houses, and Asian grocery stores. Step off the Red Line at Argyle and you're immediately in it. The gateway arch marks the start.
activities.rationale: CTA Red Line accessible — step off the train into a different world. Pho, banh mi, che desserts, and market browsing. Combine with Demera (5 blocks south) for an Ethiopian-Vietnamese double feature that's pure Chicago.

activities.name: Busy Beaver Button Museum
activities.description: A tiny free museum inside a button-making factory. Thousands of pinback buttons from the 1890s to present — political, counter-cultural, weird, funny. You can make your own button at the end.
activities.rationale: Quirky, free, and uniquely Chicago. The collection spans over a century of American culture through the lens of 1-inch buttons. Make a custom button as a souvenir. Quick stop before or after the 606 trail.
